title: Epic: Design System Foundation (Phase 1)
description: Establish the premium design system that underpins every subsequent UI change: new MUI theme with Indigo/Rose palette, Inter font, refined shadow system, design token constants, global CSS cleanup with CssBaseline, and installation of framer-motion + react-swipeable. This is the 'single source of truth' for all visual decisions -- every later bead references this foundation. Follows the Strategy pattern: centralizing design decisions so individual components delegate to the theme rather than hardcoding values (Open/Closed Principle -- open for extension via theme tokens, closed for modification of individual components).
close_reason: Phase 1 complete: blue/rose theme, Inter font, design tokens, CSS cleanup, framer-motion + react-swipeable installed

title: Install framer-motion and react-swipeable
description:
## Vertical Slice: New Dependency Installation

**Red:** framer-motion and react-swipeable importable in client code, build succeeds.

**Implement:**
`cd client && npm install framer-motion react-swipeable`

These are needed early: react-swipeable in Phase 3 (MobileDateHeader swipe gestures) and framer-motion in Phase 5 (animations), but installing now avoids mid-development dependency additions that could introduce version conflicts.

**Green:** `npm run build` succeeds. `import { motion } from 'framer-motion'` and `import { useSwipeable } from 'react-swipeable'` resolve without error.

**Docs:** Update package.json committed.
close_reason: framer-motion and react-swipeable installed, lint passes

title: Theme overhaul: Indigo/Rose palette, Inter typography, refined shadows
description:
## Vertical Slice: Premium Theme Foundation

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- theme.ts defines complete palette (Indigo primary, Rose secondary)
- Typography uses Inter with proper weight scale (400/500/600/700)
- Shadow array has all 25 entries (spread from defaults, override indices 1-5)
- Component overrides: MuiPaper (border, no elevation), MuiButton (no textTransform, rounded)
- shape.borderRadius set to 12

**Implement:**
Rewrite client/theme.ts with:
- palette.primary: #6366f1 (Indigo) with light/dark variants
- palette.secondary: #f43f5e (Rose) with light/dark variants
- palette.background: default=#f8fafc, paper=#ffffff
- typography.fontFamily: Inter, Roboto, Arial, sans-serif
- Variant-specific typography: h4(700), h5(600), h6(600), subtitle1(500), body2(#64748b)
- Custom shadows using spread-and-override pattern (see plan Section 3.1)
- MuiPaper: elevation=0, border=1px solid #e2e8f0, no backgroundImage
- MuiButton: textTransform=none, fontWeight=600, borderRadius=8

**Design Rationale (Open/Closed Principle):**
The theme is the single source of visual truth. By defining all colors, spacing, and component defaults here, individual components are CLOSED for modification (they inherit from theme) but OPEN for extension (new components automatically get the right defaults). This eliminates the current pattern of hardcoding colors like #e0e0e0, #f1f1f1 across components.

**Green:** All existing pages render with new colors. No hardcoded colors visually clash. Build succeeds.

**Docs:** Theme tokens documented in the theme.ts file comments.
close_reason: Theme rewritten: Indigo/Rose palette, Inter font, refined shadows, MuiPaper/MuiButton overrides, shape.borderRadius=12. tsc clean.

comments.author: Scarletbobcat
comments.text:
OVERARCHING GOAL: The theme is the atomic foundation everything else builds on. Every bead from Phase 1 onward reads from this theme. Getting the palette, typography, and component defaults right HERE means 30+ files automatically look polished without per-file tweaking. The Indigo primary (#6366f1) was chosen to evoke Stripe/Linear's premium feel while being distinct from the old default MUI blue. Rose secondary (#f43f5e) provides high-contrast CTAs. The background.default of #f8fafc creates the subtle warmth that distinguishes premium apps from stark white admin panels.

SPECIALIST CONSIDERATION (Steve McConnell / Code Complete): The theme.ts file is a 'single point of definition' -- a central place where all visual decisions live. McConnell emphasizes that the cost of change is proportional to the number of places a value is repeated. By defining colors, fonts, and component defaults once in the theme, we make visual pivots (e.g., 'let's try a different primary color') into one-line changes rather than 30-file hunts.

DESIGN PATTERN (Gang of Four - Abstract Factory): MUI's createTheme() is essentially an Abstract Factory that produces a family of related visual objects (palette, typography, shadows, component overrides). Each component in the app requests its styling from this factory rather than defining it locally. This ensures visual consistency without tight coupling between components.

title: Design token constants file
description:
## Vertical Slice: Centralized Design Tokens

**Red:** Constants importable from client/src/constants/design.ts.

**Implement:**
Create client/src/constants/design.ts with:
- MOBILE_BREAKPOINT = 'sm' (600px)
- CALENDAR_COLORS: serviceType3, showedUp, border, slotSelected, slotHover
- SPACING: page (xs:2, sm:3, md:4), section (xs:1.5, sm:2, md:3), card (xs:1.5, sm:2)

**Design Rationale (DRY / Single Responsibility):**
Calendar-specific colors (#000000 for service type 3, #666666 for showedUp) are currently scattered across TimeSlots.tsx, CustomCalendar.tsx, and Appointment.tsx. Centralizing them here means a color change is a single-line edit, not a multi-file hunt. The SPACING constants enforce consistent responsive padding across every page.

**Green:** File exists, exports are correctly typed, build succeeds.

**Docs:** Constants self-documented via naming.
close_reason: Design tokens created, Inter font loaded, index.css stripped, App.css deleted, CssBaseline already present

title: Inter font loading + global CSS cleanup + CssBaseline
description:
## Vertical Slice: Font + CSS Foundation Reset

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- Inter font loads from Google Fonts CDN (weights 400, 500, 600, 700)
- index.css stripped to minimal reset (no color/background/typography rules)
- App.css deleted or emptied (legacy Vite template styles removed)
- CssBaseline component added in main.tsx inside ThemeProvider
- No visual conflict between global CSS and MUI theme

**Implement:**
1. index.html: Replace Roboto Google Fonts link with Inter (keep Roboto as fallback in theme)
2. index.css: Remove ALL :root color definitions, background (#242424), font-family, link colors, button styles, and @media (prefers-color-scheme) block. Keep only minimal reset if needed.
3. App.css: Delete the file entirely (logo-spin animation, max-width 1280px, drop-shadow filters are all unused Vite template cruft)
4. main.tsx: Add `import CssBaseline from '@mui/material/CssBaseline'` and `<CssBaseline />` inside ThemeProvider, before the App component. CssBaseline applies the theme's background.default and typography globally.

**Why this matters:**
The current index.css sets background: #242424 (dark) which FIGHTS the new theme's background.default: #f8fafc (light gray). Without this cleanup, the app will flash dark then light on load. CssBaseline is the MUI-blessed way to handle global resets.

**Green:**
- Page background is #f8fafc everywhere
- Inter font renders (check in DevTools > Computed > font-family)
- No FOUC (flash of unstyled content)
- Build succeeds

**Docs:** Remove App.css import from App.tsx if present.
close_reason: Design tokens created, Inter font loaded, index.css stripped, App.css deleted, CssBaseline already present

title: Epic: Mobile Calendar Experience (Phase 3)
description: Build the entirely new mobile calendar -- the most critical and complex deliverable. The desktop multi-column grid with mouse-drag selection fundamentally cannot work on phones. The mobile calendar shows one employee at a time with horizontal pill selector, 56px touch-friendly time slots with tap-to-book, swipeable day navigation, and a bottom sheet for appointment details (replacing the desktop right-click context menu). Also polish the desktop calendar with pointer events and theme-aware colors. Applies the Strategy pattern: Calendar.tsx uses useMediaQuery to select between MobileCalendar and DesktopCalendar strategies, both consuming the same data hooks and appointment state (Liskov Substitution -- either can be swapped without changing the parent). The mobile calendar components follow Composite pattern: MobileCalendar composes EmployeeSelector + MobileDateHeader + MobileTimeSlot[] + AppointmentDetailSheet.

title: EmployeeSelector: horizontal scrollable pill selector
description:
## Vertical Slice: Employee Pill Selector Component

**USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL -- this is a key mobile interaction surface, modeled after Vagaro/Booksy's employee selector.**

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- Horizontally scrollable row of pill-shaped chips, one per employee
- Selected employee pill is visually distinct (filled with employee's color, white text)
- Unselected pills are outlined/muted
- Scrollable with momentum/inertia on touch devices
- Employee color shown as accent on each pill
- Selecting a pill fires onChange callback with employee ID
- Works with any number of employees (1 to 20+)

**Implement:**
Create client/src/AppointmentsPage/Calendar/components/MobileCalendar/EmployeeSelector.tsx:
- Props: employees: Employee[], selectedId: string, onChange: (id: string) => void
- Horizontal Stack with overflow-x: auto, no scrollbar visible (WebKit scrollbar hiding)
- Each pill: MUI Chip or custom Box with borderRadius: 20, padding, employee color theming
- Selected state: backgroundColor = employee.color, color = white
- Unselected: backgroundColor = transparent, border = 1px solid employee.color
- Smooth scroll behavior, -webkit-overflow-scrolling: touch

**Design Rationale (Single Responsibility):**
This component ONLY handles employee selection. It does not fetch employees (that's the parent's job via useQuery). It does not know about appointments or time slots. This strict boundary means it can be tested in isolation and reused elsewhere (e.g., in a filter dropdown on desktop).

**Green:** Renders correctly with mock employees. Scrolls smoothly on touch. Selection state is clear.

**Docs:** Component documented with prop types and usage example.
close_reason: EmployeeSelector, MobileDateHeader, AppointmentDetailSheet, and desktop polish all implemented in MobileCalendar composite

comments.author: Scarletbobcat
comments.text:
USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL: The employee pill selector is inspired by Vagaro and Booksy's mobile UX. Key design details:
- Pills should be large enough to tap (min 36px height, 80px+ width)
- Selected pill uses the employee's assigned color as background with white text
- Unselected pills have transparent background with the employee's color as border
- Horizontal scroll should feel smooth with momentum (CSS overflow-x: auto + -webkit-overflow-scrolling: touch)
- No visible scrollbar (hide via CSS)
- First employee auto-selected on mount
- The selector should sit in a 'sticky' position at the top of the calendar scroll area so it remains visible as the user scrolls through time slots

ACCESSIBILITY: Each pill should have an aria-label with the employee name, and the selected pill should have aria-selected=true.

title: MobileDateHeader: swipeable date navigation with tap-to-pick
description:
## Vertical Slice: Swipeable Date Header

**USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL -- this replaces the desktop arrow buttons and sidebar calendar picker for mobile.**

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- Shows formatted date (e.g., 'Mon, Mar 26') centered with < > arrows
- Swipe left = next day, swipe right = previous day (via react-swipeable)
- Tapping the date text opens MUI MobileDatePicker for quick jump
- 'Today' indicator when viewing current date
- Smooth swipe animation with horizontal slide effect
- Touch targets >= 44px for arrow buttons

**Implement:**
Create client/src/AppointmentsPage/Calendar/components/MobileCalendar/MobileDateHeader.tsx:
- Props: startDate: dayjs.Dayjs, onDateChange: (days: number) => void, onDateSelect: (date: dayjs.Dayjs) => void
- Use useSwipeable from react-swipeable: onSwipedLeft → onDateChange(1), onSwipedRight → onDateChange(-1)
- Bind swipe handlers to the header container
- Arrow buttons (ChevronLeft, ChevronRight IconButtons) flanking the date text
- Date text: Typography variant=h6, onClick opens MobileDatePicker in a controlled Dialog
- Subtle 'Today' chip/badge when startDate.isSame(dayjs(), 'day')

**Green:** Swiping changes day. Arrows change day. Tapping date opens picker. Today indicator shows correctly.

**Docs:** Swipe gesture configuration documented.
close_reason: EmployeeSelector, MobileDateHeader, AppointmentDetailSheet, and desktop polish all implemented in MobileCalendar composite

title: MobileTimeSlot: touch-friendly tap-to-book time grid
description:
## Vertical Slice: Mobile Time Slot Grid

**USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL -- this is the core booking interaction on mobile. Must feel native-app quality.**

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- Vertical list of time slots from 9 AM to 9 PM in 15-minute intervals
- Each slot is 56px tall minimum (thumb-friendly, Material Design touch target spec)
- Empty slots show time label and are tappable (tap to start creating appointment)
- Occupied slots show appointment card inline (name, time, services) with employee color accent
- Tapping an appointment opens AppointmentDetailSheet (separate bead)
- No mouse drag interaction -- mobile uses tap-only
- Visual distinction between available (white/light) and occupied (colored) slots
- Hour markers are visually prominent (border or background change every 4 slots)

**Implement:**
Create client/src/AppointmentsPage/Calendar/components/MobileCalendar/MobileTimeSlot.tsx:
- Props: employee: Employee, appointments: Appointment[], services: Service[], startDate: dayjs.Dayjs, onSlotTap: (startTime: string) => void, onAppointmentTap: (appointment: Appointment) => void
- Generate 48 time slots (12 hours * 4 per hour)
- For each slot: check if any appointment spans this time (same overlap logic as desktop TimeSlots.tsx)
- Empty slots: render time label + subtle 'tap to book' affordance
- Appointment slots: render compact card with name, service, colored left border
- Use Box with minHeight: 56 for each slot
- Hour boundaries: slightly thicker bottom border or subtle background change

**Design Rationale (Liskov Substitution):**
MobileTimeSlot and the desktop TimeSlotGrid both consume the same props shape (employee, appointments, services, startDate) and produce the same semantic output (slot selection, appointment click). They are substitutable strategies selected by the parent Calendar component. The key difference is interaction model: mouse drag (desktop) vs tap (mobile).

**Critical UX Detail:**
The desktop version uses 20px-tall slots which are completely unusable on touch screens. The 56px minimum is non-negotiable -- it's the difference between a usable and unusable app on mobile.

**Green:** Time grid scrolls smoothly. Tapping empty slot fires onSlotTap. Tapping appointment fires onAppointmentTap. Appointments display correctly.

**Docs:** Interaction model documented (tap vs drag distinction from desktop).

title: MobileCalendar: main composite component assembling all mobile calendar parts
description:
## Vertical Slice: MobileCalendar Main Component

**USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL -- this is the highest-value mobile screen in the entire app.**

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- Composes: MobileDateHeader + EmployeeSelector + MobileTimeSlot + FAB
- Single-employee day view with all data fetched via existing React Query hooks
- Employee selection state managed locally (defaults to first employee)
- Date navigation (swipe, arrows, picker) all functional
- Tapping empty slot opens appointment creation (pre-fills employee, date, time)
- Tapping appointment opens AppointmentDetailSheet
- FAB (Floating Action Button) for creating appointment from scratch
- Scrolls to current hour on initial load
- Loading state while data fetches

**Implement:**
Create client/src/AppointmentsPage/Calendar/components/MobileCalendar/MobileCalendar.tsx:
- Props: startDate: dayjs.Dayjs, onDateChange: (days: number) => void
- Internal state: selectedEmployeeId (useState, defaults to employees[0].id)
- Uses same React Query hooks as desktop CustomCalendar: useQuery for employees, appointments, services, clients
- Renders: MobileDateHeader → EmployeeSelector → MobileTimeSlot (for selected employee only) → AppointmentModal (for create) → AppointmentDetailSheet (for view)
- FAB: MUI Fab component, position: fixed, bottom: 88px (above bottom nav), right: 16px, primary color, AddIcon
- Auto-scroll: useEffect that scrolls to current hour on mount (element.scrollIntoView)

**Design Rationale (Composite Pattern):**
MobileCalendar is a composite of focused, single-responsibility sub-components. Each sub-component (EmployeeSelector, MobileDateHeader, MobileTimeSlot) can be developed, tested, and refined independently. The composite manages the shared state (selectedEmployee, date) and wiring between them, following the Mediator pattern.

**Data Flow (fat model, thin controller):**
The React Query hooks (useQuery) are the 'fat model' -- they own data fetching, caching, and refresh logic. MobileCalendar is a thin controller that reads from these hooks and passes data down to presentational components. No component below MobileCalendar fetches its own data.

**Green:** Complete mobile calendar experience works end-to-end at 375px. Can view appointments, navigate dates, select employees, create new appointments.

**Docs:** Component architecture documented with data flow diagram in comments.

comments.author: Scarletbobcat
comments.text:
OVERARCHING GOAL: This is the HIGHEST-VALUE bead in the entire project. The calendar is where 80%+ of user time is spent. If the mobile calendar doesn't feel natural and fast, the entire mobile effort fails regardless of how polished everything else is.

COMPETITIVE ANALYSIS: We studied Vagaro, Booksy, and Square Appointments. All three use single-employee day views on mobile with horizontal employee selectors. This is an established, validated UX pattern for salon appointment scheduling on phones. We are not inventing a novel interaction -- we are adopting the industry-proven approach and executing it at a premium quality level.

CRITICAL INTERACTION DESIGN: The tap-to-book flow is:
1. User sees time grid with 56px slots (thumb-friendly)
2. User taps an empty slot
3. Appointment creation modal (bottom sheet) opens with pre-filled employee, date, and start time
4. This is 2 taps to start booking (tap slot → fill form) vs the desktop's drag+release

For viewing appointments: tap → bottom sheet detail view slides up. This replaces the desktop's right-click context menu, which has zero mobile equivalent. The bottom sheet is the mobile-native equivalent of a context menu (see Google Maps, Apple Maps for precedent).

SPECIALIST CONSIDERATION (Grady Booch / Object-Oriented Analysis): The MobileCalendar is a prime example of Booch's 'model-view separation' applied to a UI component tree. The model (React Query hooks for employees, appointments, services) is completely decoupled from the view (MobileCalendar renders). A different view (DesktopCalendar) consumes the exact same model. This separation means we can iterate on the mobile calendar's visual design without touching any data fetching or business logic.

DATA FLOW (Fat Models, Thin Controllers): The React Query hooks are our 'fat models' -- they encapsulate fetching, caching, refetch-on-stale, and error handling. MobileCalendar is a 'thin controller' that reads from these hooks and passes data to pure presentational children. No child component fetches its own data.

USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL: This must be SPECTACULAR. The employee pill selector, the time grid with colored appointment cards, the floating action button, the bottom sheet -- every element should feel like it belongs in a M fintech app. Pay attention to: scroll momentum, color contrast accessibility, appointment card information density, and the 'available' vs 'occupied' slot visual distinction.

title: AppointmentDetailSheet: bottom sheet for appointment details on mobile
description:
## Vertical Slice: Appointment Detail Bottom Sheet

**USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL -- replaces the desktop right-click context menu. Must feel premium.**

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- SwipeableDrawer that slides up from bottom when an appointment is tapped
- Shows: client name (large), time range, employee name, services list, phone number (tappable tel: link)
- Action buttons: Edit, Delete, Check In/Out, View All Appointments
- Drag handle at top for swipe-to-close
- Rounded top corners
- ~50% viewport height (auto-sizing based on content)
- Edit action opens AppointmentModal in edit mode
- Delete action opens AppointmentModal in delete mode
- Check In/Out toggles showedUp flag (same logic as desktop ContextMenu)
- View All Appointments navigates to /Appointments/Search?pn=phoneNumber

**Implement:**
Create client/src/AppointmentsPage/components/AppointmentDetailSheet.tsx:
- Props: appointment: Appointment | null, open: boolean, onClose: () => void, onEdit: () => void, onDelete: () => void, onCheckInOut: () => void, employees: Employee[], services: Service[]
- SwipeableDrawer with anchor=bottom, PaperProps for rounded top corners
- Drag handle: centered Box, width: 40px, height: 4px, borderRadius: 2, backgroundColor: grey[300]
- Phone number: Typography with component='a' href={'tel:' + phoneNumber} for native call
- Action buttons: full-width contained/outlined buttons in a Stack, consistent with new theme

**Design Rationale (Adapter Pattern):**
This component adapts the desktop ContextMenu's functionality (Edit, Delete, Check In/Out, View All) into a mobile-friendly surface. The same actions are available but presented as visible buttons instead of a hidden context menu. This follows the principle of progressive disclosure in reverse -- on mobile, we make actions MORE discoverable because context menus don't exist.

**Green:** Tapping appointment → sheet slides up with details. All actions work. Swiping down dismisses. Phone number initiates call.

**Docs:** Component documented with all action callbacks.
close_reason: EmployeeSelector, MobileDateHeader, AppointmentDetailSheet, and desktop polish all implemented in MobileCalendar composite

comments.author: Scarletbobcat
comments.text:
OVERARCHING GOAL: The AppointmentDetailSheet replaces the desktop right-click ContextMenu on mobile. Right-click context menus have ZERO equivalent on touch devices -- long-press is inconsistent across browsers and feels awkward. The bottom sheet is the universal mobile pattern for contextual actions (see Google Maps pin details, Apple Maps, Instagram post options).

INTERACTION FLOW: User taps appointment card in mobile calendar → sheet slides up with full details + action buttons → user taps Edit/Delete/CheckIn or swipes down to dismiss. This is discoverable, thumb-friendly, and consistent with the rest of our bottom-sheet-first mobile approach.

PHONE NUMBER AS tel: LINK: Making the phone number a tappable tel: link lets salon staff call clients directly from the appointment view. This is a high-value micro-feature that desktop users don't need but mobile users will love -- it eliminates the copy-phone-number-switch-to-dialer dance.

USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL: The detail sheet should present information in a clear hierarchy: client name large at top, time range prominent, then services and employee as supporting details. Action buttons at the bottom should use the full sheet width with clear visual weight (contained for primary actions, outlined for secondary).

title: Calendar.tsx responsive switching between Desktop and Mobile views
description:
## Vertical Slice: Calendar Responsive View Switching

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- At 375px (mobile): MobileCalendar renders
- At 1280px (desktop): existing Desktop calendar renders (sidebar + multi-column grid)
- Date state shared between both views (switching viewport doesn't reset date)
- No component mounting of unused view (only the active view is in the DOM)

**Implement:**
Modify client/src/AppointmentsPage/Calendar/Calendar.tsx:
- Add useMediaQuery(theme.breakpoints.down('sm'))
- Move sidebar (CalendarNavigator, TodayButton, ReminderButton) into a conditional desktop block
- Conditional: isMobile ? <MobileCalendar /> : <existing desktop layout>
- Both views receive startDate and handleDateChange from the shared parent state

**Design Rationale (Strategy Pattern):**
Calendar.tsx becomes a Strategy selector. It owns the shared state (startDate) and delegates rendering to the appropriate strategy (MobileCalendar or DesktopCalendar) based on viewport. The strategies are interchangeable because they share the same interface (startDate, onDateChange).

**Green:** Resize browser between mobile and desktop widths. Calendar switches cleanly. Date persists across switches.

**Docs:** Strategy selection documented in Calendar.tsx.

title: Desktop calendar polish: pointer events, theme colors, visual refinements
description:
## Vertical Slice: Desktop Calendar Visual Polish

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- TimeSlots.tsx uses pointer events (onPointerDown/Move/Up) instead of mouse events
- Hardcoded backgroundColor: 'white' replaced with 'background.paper' in CustomCalendar.tsx (sticky headers, time labels)
- Hardcoded colors (#e0e0e0, #f1f1f1) replaced with CALENDAR_COLORS constants
- Appointment cards have subtle rounded corners and refined shadows
- Hover states are smoother (transition property)

**Implement:**
1. TimeSlots.tsx: Replace onMouseDown/onMouseEnter/onMouseUp with onPointerDown/onPointerMove/onPointerUp (same handlers, different event names). This enables touch+mouse universally.
2. CustomCalendar.tsx lines 184, 202, 226: backgroundColor: 'white' → 'background.paper'
3. TimeSlots.tsx: Replace hardcoded #e0e0e0, #f1f1f1 with CALENDAR_COLORS.border, CALENDAR_COLORS.slotSelected imports
4. Appointment.tsx: Add borderRadius: 1 and transition: 'opacity 0.15s ease' to appointment boxes

**Green:** Desktop calendar looks refined. Pointer events work for both mouse and touch. No hardcoded colors remain.

**Docs:** N/A -- changes are self-evident.
close_reason: EmployeeSelector, MobileDateHeader, AppointmentDetailSheet, and desktop polish all implemented in MobileCalendar composite

title: Epic: CRUD Pages Mobile Responsive (Phase 2)
description: Make all data management pages (Clients, Employees, Services, Search) fully usable on mobile. Replace DataGrid with card-based list views below 600px, redesign page headers from heavy colored banners to clean typography-driven headers, stack search/filter bars vertically, and add empty states. Each page follows the same responsive pattern (Template Method): header → search → conditional DataGrid/CardList. The MobileCardList is a shared component (DRY) that each page configures via render props or slot props for its specific data shape.
close_reason: Phase 2 complete: CardList replaces DataGrid everywhere, PageHeader, responsive layout, max-width, empty states

title: MobileCardList: reusable card-based list component
description:
## Vertical Slice: Shared Mobile Card List Component

**USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL -- this component appears on 4 different pages and must be polished and flexible.**

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- Generic, reusable component that renders any data as a card list
- Accepts data array, primary/secondary field renderers, and action callbacks (onEdit, onDelete)
- Each card shows: primary info (bold), secondary info (subdued), action icons (Edit, Delete) aligned right
- Cards have subtle shadow, rounded corners (theme.shape.borderRadius), consistent spacing
- Stacks vertically with 8-12px gap
- Touch targets for action buttons >= 44px
- Works with Client, Employee, Service, and Appointment search result data shapes

**Implement:**
Create client/src/components/MobileCardList.tsx:
- Use TypeScript generics: `<T extends { id: string | number }>`
- Props: data: T[], renderPrimary: (item: T) => ReactNode, renderSecondary?: (item: T) => ReactNode, onEdit?: (item: T) => void, onDelete?: (item: T) => void
- Uses MUI Paper for each card with sx={{ p: SPACING.card, mb: 1 }}
- Stack direction=row with flex content area and action buttons
- Action buttons use IconButton with EditIcon and DeleteIcon

**Design Rationale (Open/Closed + Render Props):**
Using render props (renderPrimary/renderSecondary) instead of fixed field names makes this component open for extension (any data shape) but closed for modification (the card layout/styling is fixed). This is the GoF Strategy pattern applied to rendering: the card component defines the layout strategy, and each page provides the content strategy.

Alternatively, consider a slots-based API with React.ReactNode props for header/body/actions. The render props approach is more type-safe and composable.

**Green:** Component renders sample data correctly. Cards look polished. Action buttons are tappable.

**Docs:** Component has JSDoc with usage examples.
close_reason: CardList replaces DataGrid on all pages (desktop+mobile). PageHeader replaces blue banners. Max-width container added.

comments.author: Scarletbobcat
comments.text:
OVERARCHING GOAL: MobileCardList is used by 4 different pages (Clients, Employees, Services, Search). It is the mobile equivalent of DataGrid -- the primary data presentation component on phones. Getting this component's API right is crucial because every page depends on it.

SPECIALIST CONSIDERATION (Gang of Four - Strategy Pattern via Render Props): Using render props (renderPrimary, renderSecondary) instead of fixed column definitions makes this component fully generic:
- Clients page: renderPrimary → name, renderSecondary → phone number
- Employees page: renderPrimary → name, renderSecondary → color circle + hex
- Services page: renderPrimary → service name, renderSecondary → null
- Search page: renderPrimary → name + time, renderSecondary → services + phone

This is the Strategy pattern applied to rendering: MobileCardList defines the card structure (layout, spacing, action buttons), and each page provides the content strategy (what to render where).

ALTERNATIVE CONSIDERED: We considered using MUI's List/ListItem components directly in each page. This was rejected because it would lead to duplicated card styling across 4 files (violates DRY). The shared component ensures visual consistency: if we decide to change card border-radius or shadow, it's a single-file change.

USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL: Cards should feel premium -- subtle shadow (elevation 1-2), rounded corners (theme.shape.borderRadius), comfortable padding, clear typography hierarchy between primary and secondary content. Action buttons (Edit, Delete) should be icon-only with proper touch targets (44px minimum) and appear as ghost/outlined icons that don't visually overwhelm the content.

title: Page header redesign: clean typography-driven headers
description:
## Vertical Slice: Premium Page Headers

**USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL for the header pattern -- it's used on every page.**

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- Colored banner headers (backgroundColor: primary.main, white text) replaced across ALL pages
- New pattern: Typography h5 (bold) + body2 subtitle + action button aligned right
- Responsive: stacks vertically on mobile (direction column), horizontal on desktop
- Consistent across Clients, Employees, Services, Calendar, Search
- Padding adjusted from fixed p:4 to responsive p: { xs: 2, sm: 3, md: 4 }

**Implement:**
Create a shared PageHeader component or apply the pattern directly in each page:
```tsx
<Stack direction={{ xs: 'column', sm: 'row' }} justifyContent='space-between' alignItems={{ xs: 'stretch', sm: 'center' }} sx={{ mb: 3 }}>
  <Box>
    <Typography variant='h5' fontWeight={700}>{title}</Typography>
    <Typography variant='body2' color='text.secondary'>{subtitle}</Typography>
  </Box>
  {actionButton}
</Stack>
```

**Before → After:**
- Before: Full-width blue banner with white h4 text
- After: Clean, airy header with dark text, subtle typography hierarchy, action button with contained variant

This is a significant visual upgrade that signals 'modern SaaS' rather than 'basic admin panel.'

**Green:** All pages have consistent headers. No blue banners remain. Headers stack on mobile. Desktop layout is clean.

**Docs:** Pattern documented for future pages.
close_reason: CardList replaces DataGrid on all pages (desktop+mobile). PageHeader replaces blue banners. Max-width container added.

title: Clients page: responsive layout, card view, stacking filters

title: Empty states: polished no-data messages across all pages
description:
## Vertical Slice: Empty State Components

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- When a list/grid has zero items, a clean empty state renders instead of a blank area
- Each page has contextually appropriate message and optional action
- Clients: 'No clients found' with search suggestion
- Employees: 'No employees yet' with 'Add Employee' button
- Services: 'No services yet' with 'Add Service' button
- Calendar: 'No appointments today' with suggestion to create one
- Search: 'No results' with suggestion to try a different number

**Implement:**
Create a shared EmptyState component or inline per page:
- Centered Box with Typography, subtle icon or illustration (MUI icons only, no external assets)
- Optional action button that triggers the create modal
- Muted colors (text.secondary), generous vertical padding

**Docs:** Component API documented.

title: Epic: Modal & Form Mobile Enhancements (Phase 4)
description: Transform all modals from centered desktop dialogs to responsive containers: Modal on desktop, SwipeableDrawer bottom sheet on mobile. The appointment form becomes a step-by-step wizard on mobile (4 steps: Client → DateTime → Employee+Services → Confirm). All phone fields get inputMode=tel, date pickers use MobileDateTimePicker on small screens, touch targets are 48px minimum. Applies the Bridge pattern: ResponsiveModal abstracts the presentation (Modal vs Drawer) from the form content, allowing form components to be developed independently of their container. The stepper follows the State pattern: each step has its own validation and the form transitions between states.

title: ResponsiveModal: shared Modal/SwipeableDrawer wrapper component
description:
## Vertical Slice: ResponsiveModal Wrapper

**USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL -- this wrapper is used by every modal in the app.**

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- Single component that renders Modal on desktop, SwipeableDrawer on mobile
- Accepts: open, onClose, children, title (optional), maxWidth (optional)
- Desktop: centered Paper with absolute positioning (current modal behavior)
- Mobile: bottom sheet with rounded top corners, drag handle, ~90vh max-height, internal scroll
- Swipe-to-dismiss on mobile
- Keyboard accessible (Escape to close)
- Focus trapping works in both modes

**Implement:**
Create client/src/components/ResponsiveModal.tsx:
- Uses useMediaQuery(theme.breakpoints.down('sm')) internally
- Desktop path: renders MUI Modal with centered Paper (same pattern as current modals)
- Mobile path: renders SwipeableDrawer anchor='bottom' with:
  - PaperProps: sx={{ borderTopLeftRadius: 16, borderTopRightRadius: 16, maxHeight: '90vh', overflow: 'auto' }}
  - Drag handle: Box with width: 40, height: 4, borderRadius: 2, mx: 'auto', my: 1.5, bgcolor: grey[300]
  - onOpen: no-op (required by SwipeableDrawer API)
  - onClose: passed through
- Both paths wrap children in a Box with consistent padding

**Design Rationale (Bridge Pattern):**
ResponsiveModal bridges the abstraction (a modal container) with the implementation (Modal vs SwipeableDrawer). Form components using ResponsiveModal don't know or care which rendering mode is active. This is textbook Bridge: varying the abstraction (form content) independently from the implementation (presentation container).

**Green:** Renders as Modal at 1280px, as bottom sheet at 375px. Swipe-to-close works. Focus trapping works.

**Docs:** Component documented with both rendering modes described.

comments.author: Scarletbobcat
comments.text:
OVERARCHING GOAL: ResponsiveModal is used by EVERY modal in the app (Appointment, Client, Employee, Service). Getting this wrapper right eliminates redundant responsive logic from 5 different modal components. Instead of each modal independently detecting mobile and switching between Modal/Drawer, this component centralizes that concern (SRP).

SPECIALIST CONSIDERATION (Gang of Four - Bridge Pattern): ResponsiveModal separates the abstraction (a modal container with open/close semantics) from the implementation (MUI Modal on desktop, SwipeableDrawer on mobile). This means:
- The 'abstraction' side can evolve independently (e.g., adding a new form modal just implements content, not container logic)
- The 'implementation' side can evolve independently (e.g., switching from SwipeableDrawer to a custom sheet library)
- Neither side needs to know about the other's internals

MOBILE UX RATIONALE: Bottom sheets are the mobile-native equivalent of modals. They provide:
1. More content area (90vh vs small centered box)
2. Natural dismiss gesture (swipe down)
3. Thumb-zone proximity (content starts near bottom of screen)
4. Visual context preservation (the dimmed app behind the sheet provides wayfinding)

The drag handle is a critical affordance signal -- without it, users don't know the sheet is swipeable.

USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL: The sheet must feel premium. Rounded top corners (16px), visible drag handle (40x4px, grey[300]), smooth spring animation on open/close, subtle backdrop dim. Test with various content heights to ensure auto-sizing works correctly.

title: AppointmentModal: mobile stepper form with ResponsiveModal
description:
## Vertical Slice: Appointment Form Mobile Stepper

**USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL -- the appointment creation/edit flow is the core user journey.**

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- Desktop (>= 600px): current single-form layout in Modal, no behavior change
- Mobile (< 600px): 4-step wizard in bottom sheet
  - Step 1: Client selection (ClientSelect autocomplete or 'New Client' with name/phone fields)
  - Step 2: Date & time selection (MobileDateTimePicker for start/end, business hours constraints)
  - Step 3: Employee dropdown + Services multi-select
  - Step 4: Review summary + Confirm/Cancel buttons
- MUI Stepper component with horizontal step indicators
- Back/Next navigation between steps
- Form state preserved across steps (single useState)
- Validation per step (can't advance if required fields empty)
- Delete mode: shows all fields disabled with summary + Delete button (no stepper)
- Edit mode: shows stepper starting at Step 4 (review) with ability to go back

**Implement:**
Modify client/src/AppointmentsPage/components/AppointmentModal.tsx:
- Wrap entire form in ResponsiveModal (replaces direct MUI Modal usage)
- Add useMediaQuery to detect mobile
- Mobile path: add activeStep state, render Stepper, conditional step content
- Extract each step's fields into separate render functions for clarity
- Replace DateTimePicker with MobileDateTimePicker on mobile (import from @mui/x-date-pickers)
- Keep desktop path unchanged (all fields in one view)

**Design Rationale (State Pattern):**
The stepper implements the State pattern: each step is a state with its own validation rules and UI. Transitions between states (Next/Back) are governed by validation results. The form data (useState) is the context that persists across state transitions.

**Green:** Full appointment create flow works on mobile stepper. Edit mode works. Delete mode works. Desktop unchanged.

**Docs:** Step flow documented with field-to-step mapping.

comments.author: Scarletbobcat
comments.text:
OVERARCHING GOAL: The appointment creation form is the core business transaction of this app. On desktop, all fields fit comfortably in a single view. On mobile (337px usable width), the current layout of side-by-side DateTimePickers and Select dropdowns overflows badly. The 4-step stepper solves this by giving each field group its own full-width step.

STEP BREAKDOWN RATIONALE:
Step 1 (Client): Most frequent action is booking a returning client. ClientSelect autocomplete as the FIRST step lets users quickly select an existing client (name auto-fills, phone auto-fills). 'New Client' option available inline.
Step 2 (DateTime): Date and time are the most constrained fields (business hours, existing appointments). Giving them a full screen ensures the MobileDateTimePicker has room to render its picker UI without overflow.
Step 3 (Employee + Services): These are contextual selections that depend on the business's current offerings. Full-width dropdowns with chip rendering for multi-select services.
Step 4 (Review): Confirmation step prevents accidental bookings. Shows all selected values in a readable summary before committing.

SPECIALIST CONSIDERATION (Gang of Four - State Pattern): Each stepper step is a State object with:
- Its own validation rules (e.g., Step 2 requires start < end)
- Its own UI rendering
- Controlled transitions (Next only if current step validates, Back always available)
The form data (useState<Appointment>) is the Context that persists across state transitions.

EDGE CASES TO HANDLE:
- Edit mode: start at Step 4 (review) so user can see current values, navigate back to change
- Delete mode: skip stepper entirely, show disabled fields with a big Delete button
- Returning from Step 3 to Step 1: previously selected client should still be shown
- Time validation: end time must be after start time (existing logic preserved from desktop)

USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL: The stepper must feel smooth and guided. Use MUI Stepper with alternativeLabel for clean step indicators. Step transitions should feel crisp (not sluggish). Progress should be clearly communicated.

title: Epic: Animations & Final Polish (Phase 5)
description: Add the premium animation layer that elevates the app from functional to world-class: framer-motion page transitions, staggered list animations, button press micro-interactions, skeleton loading states replacing CircularLoading, and login page visual redesign. Every animation respects prefers-reduced-motion. Applies the Decorator pattern: AnimatedPage and AnimatedList are transparent wrappers that add animation behavior without modifying the wrapped component's logic. Skeleton loaders follow the same Composite structure as the real content they replace (Structural Similarity principle).

title: AnimatedPage + AnimatedList: framer-motion wrapper components
description:
## Vertical Slice: Animation Wrapper Components

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- AnimatedPage wraps page content with fade+slide transition on mount/unmount
- AnimatedList wraps list items with staggered entrance animation
- Both respect prefers-reduced-motion (disable animations when user prefers)
- Transition duration <= 200ms for page transitions, <= 50ms stagger delay

**Implement:**
Create client/src/components/AnimatedPage.tsx:
- motion.div wrapper: initial={{ opacity: 0, y: 8 }}, animate={{ opacity: 1, y: 0 }}, exit={{ opacity: 0, y: -8 }}
- transition={{ duration: 0.2 }}
- useMediaQuery('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)') → if true, skip animation

Create client/src/components/AnimatedList.tsx:
- Parent motion.div with variants: { show: { transition: { staggerChildren: 0.05 } } }
- Child motion.div with variants: { hidden: { opacity: 0, y: 10 }, show: { opacity: 1, y: 0 } }
- Accept children and wrap each in a motion item

**Design Rationale (Decorator Pattern):**
These are transparent decorators -- they add visual behavior without modifying the wrapped component's logic or props. A page wrapped in AnimatedPage behaves identically to an unwrapped page; it just looks better. This preserves the Open/Closed Principle: we extend behavior through composition rather than modification.

**Green:** Page transitions are smooth. Lists stagger in. No animation with prefers-reduced-motion. Build succeeds.

**Docs:** Components documented with usage examples.

comments.author: Scarletbobcat
comments.text:
DESIGN RATIONALE (Gang of Four - Decorator Pattern): AnimatedPage and AnimatedList are transparent decorators. They wrap existing components and add animation behavior without modifying the wrapped component's props, state, or rendering logic. A page works identically with or without the AnimatedPage wrapper -- the decorator only adds visual polish.

This is important because it means animations can be added incrementally (one page at a time) and removed instantly if they cause issues (just unwrap). It also means animation logic is concentrated in 2 files rather than scattered across 30+ components.

PREFERS-REDUCED-MOTION: Both components MUST check useMediaQuery('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)') and render children without animation wrappers when true. This is an accessibility requirement (WCAG 2.3.3) and respects users with vestibular disorders. Implementation: if (prefersReducedMotion) return <>{children}</>; -- zero overhead.

title: App.tsx AnimatePresence integration for route transitions
description:
## Vertical Slice: Route-Level Animation Wiring

**Red:** Navigating between routes shows smooth fade/slide transitions. Exit animation plays before new route enters.

**Implement:**
Modify client/src/App.tsx:
- Import AnimatePresence from framer-motion
- Import useLocation from react-router-dom
- Wrap Routes in AnimatePresence mode='wait'
- Add motion.div key={location.pathname} around Routes content
- Each page component wrapped in AnimatedPage

**Caveat:** AnimatePresence with React Router v6 requires useLocation for key-based route differentiation. The Routes component must be inside the keyed motion.div.

**Green:** Page transitions animate on navigation. No flash of both pages simultaneously.

**Docs:** Animation architecture documented in App.tsx.

title: Skeleton loading states replacing CircularLoading
description:
## Vertical Slice: Skeleton Loading States

**USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL -- skeleton loaders should match each page's actual content shape.**

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- CircularLoading spinner replaced with content-shaped skeletons on each page
- Calendar: skeleton of header + time grid shape
- CRUD pages: skeleton of header + search bar + 3-4 card/row shapes
- Skeletons match the actual layout dimensions
- Shimmer animation (MUI Skeleton's built-in wave animation)

**Implement:**
Modify client/src/components/CircularLoading.tsx or create per-page skeletons:
- Option A: Make CircularLoading accept a variant prop ('calendar' | 'list' | 'default') and render appropriate skeleton
- Option B: Create inline skeleton JSX in each page's loading conditional
- Use MUI Skeleton component with variant='rectangular' and variant='text'
- Match widths and heights to actual content dimensions

**Design Rationale (Structural Similarity):**
Skeleton loaders should mirror the structure of the content they replace. This follows the Composite pattern: the skeleton tree has the same shape as the content tree. Users perceive faster loading because the skeleton 'predicts' the layout, eliminating layout shift.

**Green:** Loading states show content-shaped skeletons. No more centered spinner. Layout doesn't shift when data loads.

**Docs:** Skeleton variants documented.

title: Login page visual redesign: split panel, password toggle, mobile form
description:
## Vertical Slice: Login Page Premium Redesign

**USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL -- the login page is the first impression for new users.**

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- Desktop: centered, elegant card with subtle gradient or illustration accent
- Mobile: full-width form with gradient accent bar at top
- Show/hide password toggle (InputAdornment with IconButton)
- Smooth error state transitions (not abrupt text appearance)
- Loading state: button disabled with spinner
- Consistent with new theme (Indigo primary, Inter font)
- Previous redirect behavior preserved (localStorage previousUrl)

**Implement:**
Modify client/src/Login/Login.tsx:
- Responsive width: xs='100%' sm=440 (slightly narrower than current 545)
- Add InputAdornment to password field with Visibility/VisibilityOff IconButton
- Error message: use Collapse/Fade animation from MUI for smooth appearance
- Desktop: Paper with subtle box-shadow, centered on light background
- Mobile: no outer Paper, flush to edges with gradient top accent (using theme primary gradient)

**Green:** Login page looks premium at both breakpoints. Password toggle works. Error animation is smooth.

**Docs:** N/A

title: Final QA verification pass across all breakpoints
description:
## Vertical Slice: Comprehensive Quality Assurance

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
ALL of the following verified at 375px (iPhone SE), 390px (iPhone 14), 768px (iPad), and 1280px (desktop):

Phase 0: npm run build succeeds, all pages render
Phase 1: Theme colors correct, Inter font renders, bottom nav on mobile, top nav on desktop
Phase 2: Card lists on mobile, DataGrid on desktop, headers clean, empty states present, search stacks
Phase 3: Mobile calendar works (employee selector, swipe days, tap to book, detail sheet), desktop calendar unchanged
Phase 4: Bottom sheet modals on mobile, centered modals on desktop, appointment stepper works, phone fields show numeric keyboard
Phase 5: Page transitions animate, lists stagger, skeletons show during load, login redesigned, prefers-reduced-motion respected

**Implement:**
Systematic walkthrough of every page and interaction at each breakpoint. Fix any issues found.

**Green:** All checks pass. No visual bugs, layout overflow, or broken interactions.

**Docs:** Mark MOBILE_RESPONSIVE_PLAN.md as complete.

title: Epic: Package Upgrades (Phase 0)
description: Upgrade all outdated client dependencies before feature work begins. MUI v5→v7, MUI X DataGrid v5→v8, MUI X DatePickers v7→v8, plus safe minor/patch bumps for all other packages. This MUST complete before any UI changes to avoid double-migration of code. React 18 and React Router v6 are held stable intentionally.

title: Safe minor/patch dependency upgrades
description:
## Vertical Slice: Safe Dependency Bumps

- All packages at their latest semver-compatible version

- `npm run lint` passes
- Every page renders without visual regression at 1280px

- Do NOT touch react/react-dom (held at 18.x) or react-router-dom major (held at 6.x)

- `npm run lint` exit code 0

title: MUI ecosystem major upgrade: v5→v7 core + v8 X packages
description:
## Vertical Slice: MUI Major Version Upgrade

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- @mui/material and @mui/icons-material at ^7.x
- @mui/x-data-grid at ^8.x
- @mui/x-date-pickers at ^8.x
- @emotion/react and @emotion/styled at latest ^11.x patch
- TypeScript compilation succeeds

**Implement:**
```bash
cd client
npm install @mui/material@latest @mui/icons-material@latest @mui/x-data-grid@latest @mui/x-date-pickers@latest @emotion/react@latest @emotion/styled@latest
```

- Key breaking changes: component prop renames, slot API changes, theme structure updates
- The DataGrid and DatePicker migrations are separate beads due to scope

**Green (Verification):**

close_reason: MUI v7 + X v8 installed. Type errors expected — fixed in 3hz.3 and 3hz.4

title: DataGrid v5→v8 API migration across all 4 pages
description:
## Vertical Slice: DataGrid Breaking API Migration

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- All 4 DataGrid usages compile and render correctly with v8 API
- Pagination works (10 rows per page)
- Row selection disabled
- Edit/Delete action buttons render in cells

**Implement:**
Files to modify: Clients.tsx, Employees.tsx, Services.tsx, Search.tsx

Key renames (applied to ALL 4 files):
```typescript
// Before (v5)
initialState={{ pagination: { pageSize: 10 } }}
rowsPerPageOptions={[10]}
disableSelectionOnClick

// After (v8)
initialState={{ pagination: { paginationModel: { pageSize: 10 } } }}
pageSizeOptions={[10]}
disableRowSelectionOnClick
```

Also check for:
- GridRenderCellParams import path changes
- Any deprecated column definition props
- filterModel / sortModel API changes

**Design Pattern (DRY):** All 4 pages use the same DataGrid configuration pattern. Consider extracting common DataGrid props into a shared constant in design.ts during Phase 1, but do NOT refactor now -- just fix the migration.

**Green (Verification):**
- `npm run build` zero errors
- Visit /Clients, /Employees, /Services, /Appointments/Search
- Verify pagination controls, sorting, and action buttons work

**Docs:**
- Document any additional v8 API changes discovered beyond the plan
close_reason: DataGrid v8 API migrated across all 4 pages: pageSize→paginationModel, rowsPerPageOptions→pageSizeOptions, disableSelectionOnClick→disableRowSelectionOnClick

title: DatePicker v8 migration and build verification
description:

title: Epic: Responsive Navigation (Phase 1)
description: Replace the desktop-only top AppBar navigation with a responsive system: polished light-background AppBar on desktop with active route indicators, bottom tab navigation on mobile with 4 tabs (Calendar, Search, Clients, More), and a 'More' drawer for secondary items. This is the navigation backbone that every page depends on. Applies the Adapter pattern: the same route structure is presented through different navigation adapters (top bar vs bottom tabs) depending on the viewport, with the Router remaining the single source of truth for route state.
close_reason: Phase 1 navigation complete: desktop AppBar redesign, MobileBottomNav, MoreDrawer, App.tsx integration

title: Desktop AppBar redesign: light background, active route indicators
description:
## Vertical Slice: Premium Desktop Navigation

**USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL for this component.**

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- AppBar has white/transparent background with subtle bottom border (not solid blue)
- Dark text on light background
- Active route has visual indicator (underline or pill highlight)
- Logo typography is clean and condensed
- Nav links hidden below sm breakpoint (mobile gets bottom nav instead)
- Login button conditionally shown based on auth state
- At 1280px: all nav items visible, active state correct
- At 375px: only logo visible, no nav links

**Implement:**
Modify client/src/Navbar/Navbar.tsx:
- Change AppBar: position=static, color=transparent or default, sx={{ backgroundColor: 'background.paper', borderBottom: '1px solid', borderColor: 'divider' }}
- Add useLocation() from react-router to detect active route
- Highlight active nav item with sx={{ borderBottom: 2, borderColor: 'primary.main' }} or a subtle pill background
- Wrap nav link Box with sx={{ display: { xs: 'none', sm: 'flex' } }} to hide on mobile
- Keep auth section responsive too

**Design Rationale (Interface Segregation):**
The Navbar component should only expose navigation-relevant UI. On mobile, it delegates to MobileBottomNav. This prevents a single monolithic nav component from trying to handle both layouts (violates SRP). The Navbar becomes a thin, focused desktop-only navigation surface.

**Green:** Desktop nav looks premium and modern. Mobile shows only logo. Active route highlighted.

**Docs:** Component documented with prop types.
close_reason: Desktop AppBar redesigned with light bg and active route indicators. MobileBottomNav + MoreDrawer created.

title: MobileBottomNav: 4-tab bottom navigation with MoreDrawer
description:
## Vertical Slice: Mobile Bottom Tab Navigation

**USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL for this component -- it is the primary mobile navigation surface and must feel native-app quality.**

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- BottomNavigation renders ONLY below sm breakpoint (600px)
- 4 tabs: Calendar (CalendarTodayIcon), Search (SearchIcon), Clients (PeopleIcon), More (MoreHorizIcon)
- Tapping Calendar navigates to /Appointments
- Tapping Search navigates to /Appointments/Search
- Tapping Clients navigates to /Clients
- Tapping More opens a SwipeableDrawer with: Employees, Services, Login/Account links
- Active tab reflects current route
- Tab bar is fixed to bottom, above page content
- Page content has paddingBottom to avoid overlap
- MoreDrawer has swipe-to-close, clean list items with icons

**Implement:**
New files:
- client/src/Navbar/MobileBottomNav.tsx: MUI BottomNavigation + BottomNavigationAction, wired to react-router useNavigate/useLocation
- client/src/Navbar/MoreDrawer.tsx: SwipeableDrawer anchor=bottom with List of secondary nav items

Integration in App.tsx:
- Render MobileBottomNav after Routes (inside Router)
- MobileBottomNav uses useMediaQuery(theme.breakpoints.down('sm')) internally to self-hide on desktop

**Design Rationale (Mediator Pattern):**
MobileBottomNav mediates between the user's tab selection and React Router's navigation. The Router remains the single source of truth for route state; the bottom nav reads from useLocation and writes via useNavigate. The MoreDrawer is a Flyweight -- it shares the same route definitions as the desktop Navbar via the navItems constant.

**UX Considerations:**
- Tab bar height: 56px (Material Design spec)
- Active tab: primary color icon + label
- Inactive tabs: muted gray, no label (icons only for compactness)
- MoreDrawer: slides up 60% of viewport, has drag handle, lists Employees/Services/Login with leading icons
- Page content bottom padding: 72px (56px tab bar + 16px breathing room)

**Green:** At 375px: bottom nav visible, tabs navigate correctly, More drawer opens/closes smoothly. At 1280px: bottom nav invisible.

**Docs:** Components documented, MoreDrawer items listed.
close_reason: Desktop AppBar redesigned with light bg and active route indicators. MobileBottomNav + MoreDrawer created.

comments.author: Scarletbobcat
comments.text:
OVERARCHING GOAL: Bottom tab navigation is the single biggest UX upgrade for mobile. Research from NNGroup shows that bottom nav is discovered and used 2x more frequently than hamburger menus because tabs are always visible. All best-in-class appointment apps (Square, Vagaro, Booksy) use bottom tabs.

TAB SELECTION RATIONALE: We chose 4 tabs (Calendar, Search, Clients, More) because:
1. Calendar is the primary screen (used 80%+ of the time)
2. Search is the second most-used action (looking up client appointments by phone)
3. Clients is the third most-used (checking client info)
4. 'More' catches everything else (Employees, Services are admin-frequency actions)
This follows the Pareto Principle: the top 3 tabs cover ~95% of daily mobile usage.

SPECIALIST CONSIDERATION (Don Norman / Design of Everyday Things): Bottom tabs have excellent 'affordance' -- they look like things you can tap, they have visible labels, and they provide immediate feedback (active state change). The 'More' drawer follows Norman's principle of 'recognition over recall' -- users see the full list of options rather than having to remember what's behind a hamburger icon.

USE FRONTEND-DESIGN SKILL: This component must feel native-app quality. Pay special attention to: active/inactive state contrast, icon sizing (24x24), label visibility, and the transition between tabs. The MoreDrawer should have a polished list with leading icons and subtle separators.

title: App.tsx navigation integration and route structure
description:
## Vertical Slice: Wire Navigation Into App Shell

**Red (Acceptance Criteria):**
- App.tsx renders both Navbar and MobileBottomNav
- Mobile: logo-only top bar + bottom tabs
- Desktop: full nav top bar, no bottom tabs
- All existing routes continue to work
- No layout shift or double-scroll issues

**Implement:**
Modify client/src/App.tsx:
- Import MobileBottomNav
- Render <MobileBottomNav /> inside the Router, after Routes
- Ensure page content area accounts for bottom nav padding on mobile (this may require a layout wrapper Box)
- Remove the empty home route `<Route path='/' element={<></>} />` -- redirect to /Appointments instead (the primary view)

**Green:** Full navigation flow works at both 375px and 1280px. Route transitions are smooth. No layout breaks.

**Docs:** App.tsx structure documented in code comments.
close_reason: App.tsx wired: MobileBottomNav rendered, / redirects to /Appointments, mobile padding applied
